Ayahs

قَدْ سَمِعَ ٱللَّهُ قَوْلَ ٱلَّتِى تُجَـٰدِلُكَ فِى زَوْجِهَا وَتَشْتَكِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَٱللَّهُ يَسْمَعُ تَحَاوُرَكُمَآ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌۢ بَصِيرٌ (١)
Allah has certainly heard the speech of her who pleads with you about her husband and complains to Allah. Allah hears the conversation between the two of you. Indeed Allah is all-hearing, all-seeing. (1)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُظَـٰهِرُونَ مِنكُم مِّن نِّسَآئِهِم مَّا هُنَّ أُمَّهَـٰتِهِمْ ۖ إِنْ أُمَّهَـٰتُهُمْ إِلَّا ٱلَّـٰٓـِٔى وَلَدْنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَيَقُولُونَ مُنكَرًا مِّنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ وَزُورًا ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ (٢)
As for those of you who repudiate their wives by zihar, they are not their mothers; their mothers are only those who bore them, and indeed they utter an outrage and a lie. Indeed Allah is all-excusing, all-forgiving. (2)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُظَـٰهِرُونَ مِن نِّسَآئِهِمْ ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِمَا قَالُوا۟ فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَتَمَآسَّا ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ تُوعَظُونَ بِهِۦ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (٣)
Those who repudiate their wives by ³ih¡r and then retract what they have said, shall set free a slave before they may touch each other. This you are advised [to carry out], and Allah is well aware of what you do. (3)
فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيَامُ شَهْرَيْنِ مُتَتَابِعَيْنِ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَتَمَآسَّا ۖ فَمَن لَّمْ يَسْتَطِعْ فَإِطْعَامُ سِتِّينَ مِسْكِينًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لِتُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۚ وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٤)
He who cannot afford [to free a slave] shall fast for two successive months before they may touch each other. If he cannot [do so], he shall feed sixty needy persons. This, that you may have faith in Allah and His Apostle. These are Allah’s bounds, and there is a painful punishment for the faithless. (4)

Content of Surah Al-Mujadila

This Surah was revealed in Medina, and according to the nature of "Medinan" Surahs, it speaks more of legal rulings (Fiqh), the system of social life, and relations with Muslims and non-Muslims. The entirety of its discussions can be summarized into three sections:

  1. In the first section, it speaks of the ruling of "Zihar," which was considered a type of permanent divorce and separation during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah). Islam modified it and placed it on a correct path.

  2. In another section, it provides a series of instructions regarding the etiquette of gatherings, including the prohibition of "Najwa" (secret whispering) and also making room for those who have newly entered a gathering.

  3. In the final section, it presents an eloquent, detailed, and crushing discussion regarding the hypocrites—those who outwardly claimed to be Muslims but had secret dealings with the enemies of Islam. It warns true Muslims against entering the "Party of Satan" and the hypocrites, and invites them to observe "Love for the sake of Allah" and "Hate for the sake of Allah" and to join the "Party of Allah" (Hizbullah).

The naming of the Surah as "Al-Mujadila" (The Pleading Woman) is due to the expression used in its first verse.

The Virtue of Reciting Surah Al-Mujadila

Regarding the virtue of this Surah, two narrations have been cited from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

In the first narration, it is stated:

من قرأ سورة المجادلة كتب من حزب اللَّه يوم القيامة:

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Mujadila (and reflects upon it and acts according to it) will be recorded among the Party of Allah on the Day of Resurrection." And in the second Hadith, we read:

من قرا سورة الحديد و المجادلة فى صلاة فريضة و ادمنها لم يعذبه اللَّه حتى يموت ابدا، و لا يرى فى نفسه و لا فى اهله سوءا ابدا، و لا خصاصة فى بدنه:

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Hadid and Al-Mujadila in the obligatory prayers and continues to do so, Allah will never punish him throughout his entire life, and he will never see any evil in himself or his family, nor will he be afflicted by poverty or physical distress."

The harmony between the content of these Surahs and the aforementioned rewards is clear, and this itself indicates that the goal of recitation is to implement its content in life, not a recitation devoid of reflection and action.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

قَدْ سَمِعَ اللَّهُ قَوْلَ الَّتِي تُجادِلُكَ فِي زَوْجِها وَ تَشْتَكِي إِلَى اللَّهِ وَ اللَّهُ يَسْمَعُ تَحاوُرَكُما إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ بَصِيرٌ (1) الَّذِينَ يُظاهِرُونَ مِنْكُمْ مِنْ نِسائِهِمْ ما هُنَّ أُمَّهاتِهِمْ إِنْ أُمَّهاتُهُمْ إِلاَّ اللاَّئِي وَلَدْنَهُمْ وَ إِنَّهُمْ لَيَقُولُونَ مُنْكَراً مِنَ الْقَوْلِ وَ زُوراً وَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ (2) وَ الَّذِينَ يُظاهِرُونَ مِنْ نِسائِهِمْ ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِما قالُوا فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَتَمَاسَّا ذلِكُمْ تُوعَظُونَ بِهِ وَ اللَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (3) فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيامُ شَهْرَيْنِ مُتَتابِعَيْنِ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَتَمَاسَّا فَمَنْ لَمْ يَسْتَطِعْ فَإِطْعامُ سِتِّينَ مِسْكِيناً ذلِكَ لِتُؤْمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ وَ تِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللَّهِ وَ لِلْكافِرِينَ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ (4)

Occasion of Revelation

Most commentators have cited an occasion of revelation for the first verses of this Surah, the essence of which is generally the same, although they differ in details. However, this difference does not affect what we need for the commentary discussion.

The incident was as follows: A woman from the Ansar tribe named "Khawlah" (other names are also mentioned for her in other narrations), who was from the "Khazraj" tribe and whose husband was "Aws ibn al-Samit," became the target of her husband's anger in an incident. He, being a man of sharp temper and quick to react, decided to separate from her and said: "You are to me like the back of my mother" (Anti 'alayya ka-zahri ummi). This was, in fact, a type of divorce in the Age of Ignorance, but it was a divorce that was neither reversible nor did it leave the woman free to choose another husband; this was the worst state that could happen to a married woman.

Before long, the man regretted it, and since "Zihar" (saying the aforementioned sentence) was an irreversible type of divorce in the Age of Ignorance, he said to his wife: "I think you have become forbidden to me forever!" The woman said: "Do not say such a thing; go to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله and ask him the ruling on this matter." The man said: "I am ashamed." The woman said: "Then let me go." He said: "There is no objection."

The woman came to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله and recounted the incident as follows: "O Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, my husband Aws ibn al-Samit chose me as his wife when I was young, possessed beauty, wealth, and family. He consumed my wealth, my youth faded, my family dispersed, and I have grown old. Now he has performed Zihar and regretted it. Is there a way for us to return to our former life?" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "You have become forbidden to him!" She said: "O Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, he did not recite the formula of divorce, and he is the father of my children and the most beloved of all to me." He said: "You have become forbidden to him, and I have no other instruction in this regard."

The woman continuously insisted and pleaded. Finally, she turned to the threshold of God and said: "I complain to Allah of my destitution, my need, and the severity of my condition. O Allah! Send down upon the tongue of Your Prophet: 'O Allah! I complain to You of my helplessness, my need, and the severity of my state. O Allah! Send down a command to Your Prophet and resolve this problem.'"

In one narration, it is stated that the woman pleaded: "O Allah! You know my state, so have mercy on me, for I have young children; if I leave them to him, they will be lost, and if I take them myself, they will starve!" At this moment, the state of revelation came upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the opening verses of this Surah were revealed to him, clearly showing the solution to the problem of "Zihar." He said: "Call your husband." He recited the said verses to him. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked: "Can you free a slave as an expiation for Zihar?" He replied: "If I do that, nothing will remain for me." He asked: "Can you fast for two consecutive months?" He replied: "If my meal is delayed three times, my eyes fail me, and I fear I will go blind." He asked: "Can you feed sixty poor people?" He replied: "No, unless you help me." He said: "I will help you," and He helped him with fifteen Sa' (15 Man, which is the food for sixty poor people, each person receiving one Mudd, i.e., a quarter of a Man). He gave the expiation, and they returned to their former life. [1] As we said, this occasion of revelation has been cited by many commentators, including "Qurtubi," "Ruh al-Bayan," "Ruh al-Ma'ani," "Al-Mizan," "Fakhr al-Razi," "Fi Zilal al-Quran," "Abu al-Futuh al-Razi," "Kanz al-Irfan," and many other books of history and Hadith with some variations.

[1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 246 (with slight summary).

Commentary

"Zihar," an Ugly Practice of Ignorance

Given what was said in the occasion of revelation and also considering the content of the verses, the commentary on the first verses of the Surah is clear. He says: "Allah has indeed heard the speech of the woman who pleaded with you concerning her husband and complained to Allah, and He answered her request" (قَدْ سَمِعَ اللَّهُ قَوْلَ الَّتِي تُجادِلُكَ فِي زَوْجِها).

"Tujadilu" is from "Mujadala," derived from the root "Jadal," which originally means twisting a rope. Since during bilateral and insistent conversations each side wants to convince the other, "Mujadala" is applied to it.

Then He adds: "That woman, in addition to pleading with you, also complained to the threshold of Allah and requested a solution to the problem from His presence" (وَ تَشْتَكِي إِلَى اللَّهِ).

"This was while Allah heard your conversation and the insistence of that woman in solving her problem" (وَ اللَّهُ يَسْمَعُ تَحاوُرَكُما).

"Tahawur" is from the root "Hur" (rhyming with Ghur), meaning to refer back in speech or thought, and "Muhawara" is applied to bilateral conversations.

"And Allah is All-Hearing, All-Seeing" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ بَصِيرٌ).

Yes, Allah is aware of all "audibles" and "visibles" without needing organs of sight and hearing; He is present and observant everywhere, seeing everything and hearing every word.

Then He proceeds to state the ruling on Zihar. As a preamble, He crushes the root of this superstitious belief with short and decisive sentences, saying: "Those of you who perform Zihar regarding their wives (and say to their wife: 'You are to me like the back of my mother'), they are never their mothers; their mothers are only those who gave birth to them!" (الَّذِينَ يُظاهِرُونَ مِنْكُمْ مِنْ نِسائِهِمْ ما هُنَّ أُمَّهاتِهِمْ إِنْ أُمَّهاتُهُمْ إِلَّا اللَّائِي وَلَدْنَهُمْ).

Being a mother and child is not something created by speech; it is an objective external reality that is never achieved through playing with words. Therefore, even if a person says to his wife a hundred times, "You are like my mother!" she does not take the ruling of a mother, and this is a superstitious and extravagant statement.

Following that, He adds: "They utter an objectionable and ugly word, and a false and baseless speech" (وَ إِنَّهُمْ لَيَقُولُونَ مُنْكَراً مِنَ الْقَوْلِ وَ زُوراً) [1]. It is true that the speaker of this statement does not intend "reporting" (Ikhbar) but rather his intention is "enacting" (Insha'), meaning he wants to make this sentence serve as a formula for divorce. However, in any case, the content of this sentence is baseless, exactly like the superstition of "adoption" that existed in the Age of Ignorance, where they would call children their sons and apply the rulings of a son to them, which the Quran also condemned and considered a false and baseless statement, saying: ذلِكُمْ قَوْلُكُمْ بِأَفْواهِكُمْ: "That is only your saying with your mouths," and there is no reality in it (Al-Ahzab: 4).

According to this verse, "Zihar" is a forbidden and objectionable act. However, since divine obligations do not include past actions and hold authority from the moment of revelation, at the end of the verse He says: "And indeed, Allah is Pardoning and Forgiving" (وَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ).

Therefore, if a Muslim committed this act before the revelation of these verses, he should not be worried; Allah forgives him.

Some jurists and commentators believe that even now, Zihar is a forgiven sin, similar to minor sins for which Allah has promised pardon—provided major sins are avoided [1]. But there is no evidence for this meaning, and the above phrase cannot be proof of it.

However, in any case, the matter of expiation remains in full force.

In fact, this expression is similar to what appears in verse 5 of Surah Al-Ahzab, which, after prohibiting the matter of adoption, adds: وَ لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ فِيما أَخْطَأْتُمْ بِهِ وَ لكِنْ ما تَعَمَّدَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ وَ كانَ اللَّهُ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً: "There is no blame upon you for what you have done by mistake, but [only] for what your hearts intended. And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful," meaning regarding mistakes and the past.

Regarding the difference between "Afuww" and "Ghafur," some have said: "Afuww" refers to Allah's pardoning, while "Ghafur" refers to the covering of the sin, for it is possible for someone to pardon a sin but never keep it hidden, whereas Allah both pardons and conceals. Some have also interpreted "Ghufran" as covering the person from punishment, the concept of which differs from "Afw," although the result is the same.

[1]: "Zur" originally means the curvature of the upper chest, and it also means to deviate. Since a lie and false speech deviate from the truth, it is called "Zur," and for the same reason, this word is also applied to idols. [1]: "Kanz al-Irfan," Vol. 2, p. 290; an indication of this meaning is also seen in "Al-Mizan."

However, since this ugly and offensive speech was not something to be ignored by Islam, He placed a relatively heavy expiation for it to prevent its repetition, saying: "Those who perform Zihar regarding their wives and then wish to go back on what they said, must free a slave before they touch each other" (وَ الَّذِينَ يُظاهِرُونَ مِنْ نِسائِهِمْ ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِما قالُوا فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَتَمَاسَّا).

In the interpretation of the phrase "ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِما قالُوا" ("then they return to what they said"), commentators have given many possibilities, and Fadil Miqdad in "Kanz al-Irfan" has mentioned six interpretations for it. But the apparent meaning (especially considering the phrase "مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَتَمَاسَّا") is that they become regretful and repentant of their statement and intend to return to family life and sexual intercourse. In the narrations of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, this meaning has also been pointed out [1]. Other interpretations have been mentioned for this phrase that are not very suitable for the meaning of the verse and its conclusion, such as that "return" means repeating the Zihar, or that "return" means returning to the custom of the Age of Ignorance in such matters, or that "return" means to rectify and compensate for this act, and the like [2].

"Raqabah" originally means neck, but here it is an allusion to a human being, and this is because the neck is considered one of the most sensitive parts of the body, just as sometimes the word "Ra's" (head) is used to mean a person—for example, saying "five heads" instead of five people.

Then He adds: "That is what you are admonished with" (ذلِكُمْ تُوعَظُونَ بِهِ).

Do not think that such an expiation for "Zihar" is a heavy and unbalanced one, for this is a cause for admonition, awakening, and the training of your souls, so that you can control yourselves against such ugly and forbidden acts.

In principle, all expiations have a deterrent and educational aspect, and perhaps expiations that have a financial aspect have more impact than most disciplinary punishments (Ta'zirat) that have a physical aspect.

And since some might evade the expiation with excuses and have sexual intercourse with their wives after Zihar without paying the expiation, at the end of the verse He adds: "And Allah is Aware of what you do" (وَ اللَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ).

He is aware of the Zihar, the abandonment of expiation, and your intentions!

Also, since freeing a slave is not possible for all people—as we saw in the occasion of revelation where "Aws ibn al-Samit," regarding whom these verses were first revealed, said to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "I am not able to pay such a heavy expiation, and if I do so, I must lose all my possessions"—and it is also possible for a person to be financially able to free a slave but no slave is found for this purpose, as is the case in our era; therefore, the universality and eternity of Islam necessitates that in the next stage, a substitute for freeing slaves be mentioned. For this reason, in the next verse He says: "And whoever does not find [a slave] must fast for two consecutive months before they touch each other" (فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيامُ شَهْرَيْنِ مُتَتابِعَيْنِ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَتَمَاسَّا).

This expiation also has a deep deterrent effect; furthermore, since fasting has a profound effect on purifying the spirit and refining the soul, it can prevent the repetition of such acts in the future.

Of course, the apparent meaning of the verse is that all sixty days should be performed consecutively, and many Sunni jurists have also issued fatwas according to it. However, in the narrations of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, it is stated that if he fasts a little of the second month (even one day) following the first month, it will be an instance of "two consecutive months," and this clarification governs the apparent meaning of the verse [1]. This shows that the meaning of "consecutive" (Tatur) in the above verse and verse 92 of Surah An-Nisa (expiation for accidental killing) is being consecutive in general, and of course, such an interpretation is only heard from the Infallible Imam عليه السلام who is the heir to the knowledge of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and this type of fasting is a facilitation for the duty-bound (more explanation on this subject should be studied in books of jurisprudence in the Book of Fasting, and the chapters on Zihar, and expiation for accidental killing) [2]. Additionally, the meaning of the phrase "فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ" ("whoever does not find") is not that he absolutely has nothing in his possession, but rather it means he has nothing beyond the needs and necessities of life with which he could buy and free a slave.

And since many people are also unable to perform the second expiation, i.e., fasting for two consecutive months, He mentions another substitute for it, saying: "And whoever is unable to fast for two consecutive months, then the feeding of sixty poor people" (فَمَنْ لَمْ يَسْتَطِعْ فَإِطْعامُ سِتِّينَ مِسْكِيناً).

The apparent meaning of "feeding" is to give enough food to satisfy them in one meal, but in Islamic narrations, it is determined as one "Mudd" of food (a quarter of a Man or about 750 grams), although some jurists have determined it as equivalent to two Mudds (one and a half kilograms) [1]. Then, in the continuation of the verse, He once again points to the main goal of such expiations, adding: "This is so that you may believe in Allah and His Messenger" (ذلِكَ لِتُؤْمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ).

Yes, compensating for sins through expiations strengthens the foundations of faith and makes a person committed to divine regulations both intellectually and practically! And at the end of the verse, so that all Muslims take this matter seriously, He says: "These are the limits of Allah, and for the disbelievers is a painful punishment" (وَ تِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللَّهِ وَ لِلْكافِرِينَ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ).

It should be noted that the word "Kufr" (disbelief) has various meanings, one of which is "practical disbelief," meaning disobedience and sin, and in the verse under discussion, this very meaning is intended, just as in verse 97 of Surah Al-Imran regarding those who do not perform the Hajj pilgrimage, He says: وَ لِلَّهِ عَلَى النَّاسِ حِجُّ الْبَيْتِ مَنِ اسْتَطاعَ إِلَيْهِ سَبِيلًا وَ مَنْ كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَنِيٌّ عَنِ الْعالَمِينَ: "And [due] to Allah from the people is a pilgrimage to the House—for whoever is able to find thereto a way. But whoever disbelieves (and abandons Hajj)—then indeed, Allah is free from need of the worlds."

"Hadd" (limit) means something that acts as a barrier between two things, and therefore the borders of countries are called "Hudud." Divine laws are called divine limits because crossing them is not permitted. We had a more detailed explanation in this regard in Volume 1 under verse 187 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

[1]: Refer to "Majma' al-Bayan" under the verses discussed. [2]: Refer to "Kanz al-Irfan," Vol. 2, p. 290 and "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 247. [1]: Refer to "Wasa'il al-Shi'a," Vol. 7, p. 271 (Chapter 3 of the chapters on the remainder of obligatory fasting). [2]: Whenever the intention is the consecutiveness of two months, not the consecutiveness of all their days, as soon as one starts the second month, this type of consecutiveness is achieved (take note). [1]: The famous view among our jurists (as we said) is one "Mudd," and the evidence for it is many narrations that perhaps reach the level of Tawatur; some are regarding expiation for accidental killing, some for expiation of an oath, and some for expiation of the month of Ramadan, combined with the fact that none of the jurists have made a distinction between the types of expiations. However, it is narrated from the late Sheikh Tusi in "Al-Khilaf," "Al-Mabsut," "Al-Nihayah," and "Al-Tibyan" that its amount is two Mudds, and in this regard, he argued with the narration of Abu Basir which appears regarding the expiation of Zihar and sets its limit at two Mudds. But this narration must either be specific to the expiation of Zihar, or if we accept that the jurists have not made a distinction between expiations—as is truly the case—it must be interpreted as a recommendation (Istihbab).

Some Rulings on Zihar

  1. "Zihar," which is mentioned in two verses of the Holy Quran (the verse under discussion and verse 4 of Surah Al-Ahzab), was among the ugly practices of the Age of Ignorance. When a man became upset with his wife, in order to put her in a strait and pressure, he would say: "Anti 'alayya ka-zahri ummi" (You are to me like the back of my mother) [1]. Following that, they believed that the woman became forbidden to her husband forever, and she could not even choose another husband! Thus she remained in limbo. Islam condemned this matter—as we saw—and issued the command for expiation regarding it.

Therefore, whenever someone performs Zihar regarding his wife, his wife can refer to the religious judge (Hakim al-Shar') to obligate him to either officially separate from her through divorce or return to married life. However, before returning, he must give the expiation we read in the above verses: that is, if able, free a slave; if unable, fast for two consecutive months; and if that too is not possible, feed sixty poor people. This means this expiation is not optional (Takhyiri) but rather sequential (Tartibi).

  1. Zihar is among the major sins, and the tone of the above verses is a clear witness to this matter. The view of some who count it among the minor sins and consider it pardoned is an incorrect view.

  2. Whenever someone is unable to pay the expiation at any stage, can he suffice with only repentance and seeking forgiveness and return to married life? There is a difference of opinion among jurists. A group, relying on a Hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام [1], believe that in other expiations, repentance and seeking forgiveness are sufficient when there is no ability, but in the expiation of Zihar, it is not sufficient, and they must be separated through divorce.

Meanwhile, another group believes that here too, seeking forgiveness and repentance substitute for the expiation, and their evidence is another narration cited from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام in this regard [2].

Some also believe that if possible, fasting for eighteen days is sufficient [1]. Reconciling the narrations is also not unlikely, in such a way that in case of inability in any form or shape, he can seek forgiveness and return to married life, although it is recommended (Mustahabb) that in such a case he separate from his wife (for such a reconciliation, given the reliability of the chains of both Hadiths, is a recognized reconciliation and has many parallels in jurisprudence).

  1. Many jurists believe that if he performs Zihar several times (meaning he repeats the said sentence with serious intent), he must give several expiations even if it occurs in a single gathering, unless his intention in repeating was emphasis, not a new Zihar.

  2. Whenever he has sexual intercourse with his wife before giving the expiation, he must give two expiations: one expiation for the Zihar, and one expiation for the sexual intercourse before giving the expiation of Zihar. This ruling is agreed upon among the jurists; of course, the above verses are silent on this matter, but it has been pointed out in the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام [2].

  3. Islam's decisive encounter with the matter of Zihar expresses the reality that Islam never allows the rights of women to be violated by self-willed men using oppressive customs and habits; rather, it breaks every wrong and superstitious tradition in this regard, no matter how firm it may be among the people.

  4. Freeing a slave, which is the first expiation for Zihar, in addition to having an interesting proportion to the issue of fighting against the enslavement of women in the clutches of self-willed men, shows that Islam wants to end the slavery of slaves through all possible ways. Therefore, this matter has been introduced not only in the expiation of "Zihar" but also in the expiation of "accidental killing," as well as in the expiation of "the fast of the month of Ramadan" (for one who has intentionally broken the fast), and also the expiation for "violating an oath" or "breaking a vow." This itself is an effective means for realizing the program of the final liberation of slaves.

[1]: "Zahr" in the above phrase, as some commentators have said, does not mean "back," but is rather an allusion to the relationship that results from the state of marriage. Therefore, the meaning of the sentence becomes: "Marriage with you is like the marriage of my mother" (Refer to Lisan al-Arab under the root "Zahr" and the Tafsir al-Kabir of Fakhr al-Razi). [1]: "Wasa'il al-Shi'a," Vol. 15, p. 554 (Hadith 1 from Chapter 6). [2]: Same source, p. 555, Hadith 4. [1]: "Kanz al-Irfan," Vol. 2, p. 292. [2]: "Wasa'il al-Shi'a," Vol. 15, p. 526 (Hadiths 1, 3, 4, 5, and 6).

Ayahs

Commentary

Those Who Oppose Allah

Since the final sentence of the previous verses warned everyone to observe the Divine limits and not to transgress them,## Commentary

Those Who Oppose Allah

Since the final sentence of the preceding verses warned everyone to observe the Divine limits and not to transgress them, the verses under discussion speak of those who have not only transgressed these limits but have risen to oppose Allah and His Messenger (peace be upon him and his family), clarifying their fate in this world and the hereafter.

First, He says: "Indeed, those who oppose Allah and His Messenger will be abased just as those before them were abased" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُحَادُّونَ اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ كُبِتُوا كَما كُبِتَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ).

"Yuhaddun" (يُحَادُّونَ) is derived from the root "muhaddah," which means armed struggle and the use of "hadid" (iron), though it is also applied to non-armed opposition.

Some have said that "muhaddah" originally means prevention, derived from the root "hadd," which refers to a barrier between two things; hence, a gatekeeper is called a "haddad." Both meanings are close in terms of the ultimate result, even if they are derived from two different roots.

"Kubitu" (كُبِتُوا) is from the root "kabt" (rhyming with "sabt"), meaning to prevent with force and to humiliate. This indicates that Allah has decreed humiliation and disgrace as the punishment for those who rise to oppose Him and His Messenger, and He deprives them of His boundless grace [^1]. This expression is similar to the one found in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 114, regarding those who prevent people from the mosques and the worship of Allah and rise to oppose the true religion: لَهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيا خِزْيٌ وَ لَهُمْ فِي‌ الآخرة عذاب عظيم—"For them is disgrace in this world, and for them in the Hereafter is a great punishment."

Or what is mentioned in Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 33, regarding those who wage war against Allah and His Messenger and spread corruption on earth: ذلِكَ لَهُمْ خِزْيٌ فِي الدُّنْيا وَ لَهُمْ فِي‌ الآخرة عذاب عظيم—"That is for them a disgrace in this world, and for them in the Hereafter is a great punishment."

Then He adds: "And We have certainly sent down clear signs" (وَ قَدْ أَنْزَلْنا آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ).

Therefore, the proof has been sufficiently established, and no excuse or pretext remains for opposition; nonetheless, if they oppose, they must be punished.

Not only are they punished in this world, but "for the disbelievers is a humiliating punishment in the Resurrection" (وَ لِلْكافِرِينَ عَذابٌ مُهِينٌ).

In this way, the previous sentence referred to their worldly punishment, and this sentence refers to their punishment in the Hereafter. The evidence for this meaning is the phrase "just as those before them were abased" (كَما كُبِتَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ), and the following verse also testifies to this.

In any case, this Divine threat was fulfilled regarding those who stood against the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and the Quran; they faced humiliation and defeat in the battles of Badr, Khaybar, Khandaq, and others, and finally, the Conquest of Mecca folded the scroll of their power and grandeur, and Islam became victorious everywhere.

The next verse describes the time when their punishment in the Hereafter will occur: "On the Day Allah will resurrect them all and inform them of what they did" (يَوْمَ يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللَّهُ جَمِيعاً فَيُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما عَمِلُوا) [^1]. Yes, "Allah has enumerated it, while they forgot it" (أَحْصاهُ اللَّهُ وَ نَسُوهُ). For this reason, when their eyes fall upon the record of their deeds, they will cry out: ما لِهذَا الْكِتابِ لا يُغادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَ لا كَبِيرَةً إِلَّا أَحْصاها—"What is this book that leaves nothing small or great except that it has enumerated it?" (Al-Kahf: 49).

And this is itself a painful punishment—that Allah brings back to their memory their forgotten sins, and they are disgraced before the creation in the scene of the Gathering.

At the end of the verse, He says: "And Allah is Witness over all things" (وَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ شَهِيدٌ).

This is, in fact, the evidence for what was mentioned in the previous sentence.

Indeed, Allah's presence everywhere, at all times, and within and without us, necessitates that He enumerates not only our actions but also our intentions and beliefs, and on that Great Day, which is the "Day of Manifestation," He will recount them all, so that both the person himself and others may know the reason if there is a severe punishment and penalty involved.

Then, to emphasize Allah's presence everywhere and His knowledge of everything, the discourse turns to the issue of "najwa" (secret whispers), saying: "Do you not see that Allah knows whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ). Although the address here is to the Prophet, the intended audience is all people [^1], and this is, in fact, a prelude to explaining the issue of secret whispers and private conversations.

Then He adds: "There is no private conversation of three except that He is the fourth of them, nor of five except that He is the sixth of them" (ما يَكُونُ مِنْ نَجْوى‌ ثَلاثَةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ رابِعُهُمْ وَ لا خَمْسَةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ سادِسُهُمْ) [^2] "nor of less than that or more except that He is with them wherever they are" (وَ لا أَدْنى‌ مِنْ ذلِكَ وَ لا أَكْثَرَ إِلَّا هُوَ مَعَهُمْ أَيْنَ ما كانُوا).

"Then He will inform them of what they did on the Day of Resurrection. Indeed, Allah is Knowing of all things" (ثُمَّ يُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما عَمِلُوا يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عَلِيمٌ).

In this verse, several points attract attention:

1- "Najwa" and "najat" originally mean a high place that is separated from its surroundings due to its elevation. Since a person who does not want others to know of his words goes to a spot separated from others, secret whispers are called "najwa." Alternatively, it may be because the one whispering wants to "save" (najat) his secrets from being accessible to others.

2- Some believe that "najwa" must necessarily be between three or more people, and if there are two, it is called "sarar." However, this contradicts the apparent meaning of the verse itself, as the phrase "nor of less than that" refers to fewer than three, i.e., two people. Of course, when two people whisper together, a third person must be present nearby; otherwise, there would be no need for whispering. But this point is unrelated to what we have stated.

3- It is noteworthy that in the above verse, He first speaks of a whisper of three and then of five, but no mention is made of a whisper of four, which lies between the two. Although all of these are by way of example, some commentators have mentioned various reasons for this, the most appropriate of which is the observance of eloquence in speech and the avoidance of repetition. For if He had said, "Every three people who sit to whisper, Allah is their fourth, and every four people who sit to whisper, Allah is their fifth," the number "four" would have been repeated, which is far from eloquence. Some have also said that these verses were revealed concerning a group of hypocrites who were exactly of these numbers, namely three and five people.

4- The meaning of Allah being the fourth or sixth of them is that He is present and observant everywhere and is aware of everything; otherwise, His Holy Essence has no place, nor is He ever described by numbers. His Oneness is also not a numerical unity, but rather means that He has no peer, likeness, or equal.

5- It is also noteworthy that at the end of the verse, the discourse goes beyond "najwa" and says: "Allah is with the people everywhere and will inform them of their deeds on the Day of Resurrection," ending the verse with Allah's encompassing knowledge, just as the verse began with His encompassing knowledge of all things.

6- Some commentators have cited a cause of revelation for this verse from Ibn Abbas, stating that it was revealed concerning three individuals named Rabi'ah, Habib, and Safwan. They were talking together; one said to the other, "Does Allah know what we are saying?" The second said, "He knows some and does not know some!" The third said, "If He knows some, He knows all!" (The above verse was revealed and announced that Allah is present in every secret whisper and is aware of everything in the earth and the heavens, so that these blind-hearted, heedless ones might realize their mistake) [^1].

Key Point: Allah's Presence in Every Secret Whisper

As we have said, Allah is neither a body nor does He have physical attributes; for this reason, time and space cannot be conceived for Him. Yet, at the same time, if we imagine there could be a place where Allah is not present and observant, we have limited Him.

In other words, He has encompassing knowledge of everything even though no place can be conceived for Him. Furthermore, His angels are present everywhere, hearing and recording all deeds and words. Therefore, in a narration from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), regarding the interpretation of this verse, we read:

"He only intended by that the dominance of His trustees through the power He has instilled in them over all His creation, and that their action is His action." [^1]

Of course, this is one dimension of the matter. In another dimension, the presence of Allah's Essence is considered, as we read in another narration that one of the great Christian scholars asked the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him): "Where is Allah?" He replied:

"He is here and here, and above and below, and encompassing us and with us, and that is His saying: 'There is no private conversation of three except that He is the fourth of them...'" [^2]

And in the famous "Ihliljah" narration from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), we read: "The reason Allah is named 'All-Hearing' (Sami') is because three people do not whisper together except that He is the fourth of them..." Then he added:

"He hears the crawling of the ant upon the smooth stone! And the flapping of the bird's wings in the air! Nothing hidden is concealed from Him, nor anything perceived by ears and eyes, nor what is not perceived by ears and eyes—whether it be great or fine, small or large." [^1]

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the first verse under discussion, two occasions of revelation have been narrated, each relating to a specific part of the verse:

First: A group of Jews and hypocrites used to engage in private conversations (najwa) among themselves, separate from the believers. They would whisper to one another and sometimes make distressing gestures with their eyes toward the believers. When the believers saw this scene, they said: "We think distressing news has reached them regarding our relatives and loved ones who have gone to Jihad, and that is what they are talking about." This caused grief and sorrow for the believers. When they repeated this behavior, the believers complained to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered that no one should engage in private conversation in the presence of Muslims, but they did not listen and repeated it again. Thus, the above verse was revealed (and severely threatened them for this act). [^1]

In the Sahih of Bukhari and Muslim, and many books of Tafsir, it is narrated that a group of Jews came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and instead of saying "As-Salamu Alayk" (Peace be upon you), they said: "As-Samu Alayk, O Aba al-Qasim" (which means "Death be upon you" or "Weariness and exhaustion be upon you"). The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله replied to them: "Wa Alaykum!" (And upon you!). Aisha says: "I noticed this and said: 'Alaykum as-Sam, and may Allah curse you and be angry with you!'" The Prophet said to me: "Be gentle and avoid harshness and foul language." I said: "Did you not hear them saying 'Death be upon you'?" He replied: "Did you not hear that I answered 'And upon you'?" It was then that Allah revealed the above verse, stating that when this group comes to you, they greet you with a greeting that Allah has not greeted you with. [^2]

Commentary

Is Private Conversation from Satan?

The discussion in these verses continues the topic of private conversation (najwa) mentioned in the previous verses.

First, He says: "Have you not seen those who were forbidden from private conversation, then they return to what they were forbidden from, and they converse privately for sin and aggression and disobedience to the Messenger?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ نُهُوا عَنِ النَّجْوى‌ ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِما نُهُوا عَنْهُ وَ يَتَناجَوْنَ بِالْإِثْمِ وَ الْعُدْوانِ وَ مَعْصِيَةِ الرَّسُولِ‌).

From this expression, it is clearly understood that they had been warned previously to refrain from najwa—an act that inherently produces suspicion and anxiety in others. However, they not only ignored this command but chose the content of their private conversations to be sin and matters contrary to the commands of Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

The difference between "sin" (ithm), "aggression" (udwan), and "disobedience to the Messenger" (ma'siyat al-rasul) is that "sin" includes transgressions of an individual nature (such as drinking wine), "aggression" refers to matters that cause encroachment upon the rights of others, and "disobedience to the Messenger" relates to the orders issued by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself as the head of the Islamic government regarding the interests of the Muslim society.

Therefore, in their private conversations, they discussed every kind of wrongful act, whether related to themselves, others, the Islamic government, or the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

The use of the terms "return" (ya'udun) and "converse privately" (yatanajun) in the present continuous tense indicates that they repeated this act constantly, and their intention was to cause distress in the hearts of the believers.

In any case, the above verse, as a report of the unseen, pulled back the curtain on their wrongful deeds and exposed their deviant path.

Continuing this discourse, it points to another wrongful act of the hypocrites and Jews, adding: "And when they come to you, they greet you with a greeting that Allah has not greeted you with" (وَ إِذا جاؤُكَ حَيَّوْكَ بِما لَمْ يُحَيِّكَ بِهِ اللَّهُ‌).

"Hayyawka" is derived from the root "tahiyyah," which originally comes from "hayat" (life), meaning to pray for the health and life of another.

The "Divine Greeting" referred to in this verse is the phrase "As-Salamu Alayk" (or "Salamullahi Alayk"), similar versions of which appear repeatedly in the verses of the Quran regarding the Prophets, the people of Paradise, and others. For instance, in Surah As-Saffat, verse 181, we read: "And peace be upon the messengers" (وَ سَلامٌ عَلَى الْمُرْسَلِينَ‌).

However, the greeting that Allah had not spoken and was not permissible was the phrase "As-Samu Alayk" (Death be upon you, or weariness be upon you). There is also the possibility that it refers to the greeting of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), where they would say: "An'im Sabahan" (May your morning be pleasant) and "An'im Masa'an" (May your evening be pleasant), without any attention to God in their speech or seeking health and goodness from Him for the other party.

Although this was common in the Jahiliyyah, its prohibition is not established, and interpreting the verse in this way is unlikely.

Then it adds: They not only commit these great sins but are so intoxicated with the wine of arrogance that "they say within themselves: 'If our deeds are evil and Allah knows it, then why does He not punish us for what we say?'" (وَ يَقُولُونَ فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ لَوْ لا يُعَذِّبُنَا اللَّهُ بِما نَقُولُ‌).

In this way, they proved both their lack of faith in the Prophethood of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and their lack of faith in the all-encompassing knowledge of Allah.

But the Quran answers them in one short sentence: "Hell is sufficient for them; there is no need for another punishment. The very Hell they will soon enter—and what a wretched destination it is!" (حَسْبُهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ يَصْلَوْنَها فَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ).

Of course, this expression does not negate worldly punishment for them, but it clearly states this reality: if there were no punishment other than the torment of Hell, it would suffice for them, and they will see the punishment for all their deeds collectively in Hell.

Since believers also occasionally engaged in private conversations due to necessities or inclinations, the next verse addresses them. To ensure they do not become tainted with the sins of the hypocrites and Jews in this act, it says: "O you who have believed, when you converse privately, do not converse for sin and aggression and disobedience to the Messenger; the content of your private conversation must be pure and divine. Converse for righteousness and piety" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا تَناجَيْتُمْ فَلا تَتَناجَوْا بِالْإِثْمِ وَ الْعُدْوانِ وَ مَعْصِيَةِ الرَّسُولِ وَ تَناجَوْا بِالْبِرِّ وَ التَّقْوى‌).

"And fear Allah, the One to whom you shall all be gathered" (وَ اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ الَّذِي إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ‌).

From this expression, it is clearly understood that the principle of najwa is permissible if it occurs among believers, does not arouse suspicion, does not produce anxiety, and its content is the recommendation of goodness and virtues.

However, whenever it originates from people like the Jews and hypocrites whose goal is to harass pure-hearted believers, the act itself is ugly and forbidden, let alone if its content is also satanic.

Therefore, in the next verse, which is the last verse under discussion, it adds: "Private conversation is only from Satan" (إِنَّمَا النَّجْوى‌ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ‌). "So that those who believe may be grieved" (لِيَحْزُنَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا).

"But they should know that Satan cannot harm the believers at all except by the permission of Allah" (وَ لَيْسَ بِضارِّهِمْ شَيْئاً إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ‌).

This is because every cause in the universe has its effect by the command of Allah; even the burning of fire and the sharpness of a sword—if the Majestic One forbids it, they will not even obey the command of the Friend (Khalil).

"Therefore, the believers must put their trust only in Allah, fear nothing but Him, and set their hearts on none other than Him" (وَ عَلَى اللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

With the spirit of trust and reliance on Allah, they can successfully overcome all these problems, neutralize the plans of Satan's followers, and crush their conspiracies.

Key Points

1- Types of Private Conversation and Whispering

In Islamic jurisprudence, this act has different rulings depending on the circumstances, categorized into the five rulings (al-ahkam al-khamsah). That is, it is sometimes forbidden (haram), and this is when it causes distress, harassment, or the violation of a Muslim's dignity (as mentioned in the above verses). Such najwa is a satanic conversation aimed at grieving the believers.

Conversely, it sometimes takes the ruling of obligation (wujub), and this is when there is a necessary secret whose disclosure is dangerous, and failing to mention it would lead to the loss of a right or a danger to Islam and Muslims.

At other times, it is characterized as recommended (mustahabb), such as when a person seeks it to perform good deeds, righteousness, and piety. It can also be disliked (makruh) or permissible (mubah).

However, fundamentally, if there is no important goal involved, engaging in private conversation is not a commendable act and is contrary to the etiquette of a gathering, as it is considered a form of indifference or distrust toward others. Thus, we read in a hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "When you are three people, two of you should not converse privately excluding the third companion, for that grieves him." [^1]

In another hadith from Abu Sa'id (al-Khudri), he says: "We used to take turns keeping watch near the Prophet's residence at night to carry out his orders should something necessary arise. One night, a large number of people gathered and were speaking softly. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله came out and said: 'What is this private conversation? Have you not been forbidden from najwa?'" [^2]

It is also understood from several narrations that Satan uses every means to grieve the believers, not just najwa. Sometimes, in the world of dreams, he visualizes scenes before their eyes that cause them sorrow. Believers have been instructed in such cases to repel these satanic suggestions by seeking refuge in the Pure Essence of Allah and trusting in Him. [^3]

2- What is the Divine Greeting?

Usually, when entering gatherings, people say words to one another that indicate respect and affection, calling it "tahiyyah" (greeting). It is understood from the above verses that the greeting must also have a divine aspect, just as all customs and manners of social interaction should be. In a greeting, in addition to honoring and respecting the other party, there should be a remembrance of Allah, just as in "Salam," we request the health and safety of the person from Allah.

In the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, regarding the verses under discussion, it is mentioned that a group of the Prophet's companions, when they came to him, would say: "An'im Sabahan" and "An'im Masa'an" (May your morning/evening be pleasant). This was the greeting of the Age of Ignorance. The Quran forbade this, and the Messenger of Allah said to them: "Allah has commanded us with a greeting better than that, which is the greeting of the people of Paradise: 'As-Salamu Alaykum,'" [^1] which means "Salamullahi Alaykum" (The peace of Allah be upon you).

The distinction of the Islamic "Salam" is that, on one hand, it is coupled with the remembrance of Allah, and on the other hand, it encompasses the safety of everything—including religion, faith, body, and soul—not just comfort, luxury, and ease. [^2] Regarding the ruling of greeting and "Salam" and its etiquette, we had a detailed discussion under verse 86 of Surah An-Nisa (refer to Volume 4 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 41 onwards).

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا قِيلَ لَكُمْ تَفَسَّحُوا۟ فِى ٱلْمَجَـٰلِسِ فَٱفْسَحُوا۟ يَفْسَحِ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ۖ وَإِذَا قِيلَ ٱنشُزُوا۟ فَٱنشُزُوا۟ يَرْفَعِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنكُمْ وَٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ دَرَجَـٰتٍ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (١١)
O you who have faith! When you are told, ‘Make room,’ in sittings, then do make room; Allah will make room for you. And when you are told, ‘Rise up!’ Do rise up. Allah will raise in rank those of you who have faith and those who have been given knowledge, and Allah is well aware of what you do. (11)

Occasion of Revelation

"Tabarsi" in Majma' al-Bayan and "Alusi" in Ruh al-Ma'ani, along with a group of other commentators, have narrated that one Friday, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was sitting in the "Suffah" (a large platform located next to the Prophet's mosque). A group was present with him, and the space was cramped. It was the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله habit to show great respect to the veterans of the Battle of Badr, whether from the Muhajirun or the Ansar. At that moment, a group of the people of Badr arrived, while others had already filled the space around the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. When they reached the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, they offered their greetings (Salam). The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله responded, and then they greeted those present, who also responded. They remained standing, waiting for those present to make room for them, but no one moved!

This was difficult for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to witness. He turned to some of those sitting around him and said: "O so-and-so, and so-and-so, stand up." He asked several people to rise so that the newcomers could sit (this was, in fact, a form of teaching etiquette and a lesson in respecting those foremost in faith and Jihad).

However, this was unpleasant for the few who had to stand up, to the extent that the effects were visible on their faces. The hypocrites (who seized every opportunity) said: "The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did not observe the rule of justice; he made those who were sitting lovingly near him stand up for the sake of individuals who entered the gathering later." At this point, the above verse was revealed (explaining part of the etiquette of sitting in gatherings to them). [^1]

Commentary

Respecting Predecessors in Gatherings

Following the instruction given in the previous verses regarding the abandonment of private conversation (Najwa) in gatherings and limiting it to specific cases, this verse mentions another etiquette of the gathering, stating: "O you who have believed, when you are told, 'Make space in the assemblies' and give room to newcomers, then obey."

[^1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 28, p. 25; and Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 252. (Other commentators such as Fakhr Razi, Qurtubi, and Suyuti in Al-Durr al-Manthur, and the author of Fi Zilal, have also narrated the same point with slight differences under the verse in question).

(يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا قِيلَ لَكُمْ تَفَسَّحُوا فِي الْمَجَالِسِ فَافْسَحُوا) [^1] "For if you do so, Allah will expand your place in Paradise, and in this world, He will also grant expansion to your heart, soul, and provision" (يَفْسَحِ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ).

"Tafassahu" is derived from the root "Fas-h" (rhyming with qufl), meaning a wide place. Therefore, "Tafassuh" means to expand or make room. This is one of the etiquettes of a gathering: when a newcomer enters, those present should sit closer together and make room for him, lest he be left wandering, or perhaps feel tired or embarrassed. This matter is one of the means of strengthening the bonds of love and friendship, unlike private conversation (Najwa) mentioned in the previous verses, which is a factor for hatred, suspicion, and enmity.

It is noteworthy that the Holy Quran—this great Heavenly Book which serves as the constitution for Muslims—does not leave even many details of moral issues and collective living unsaid. Amidst important and fundamental instructions, it points to these details so that Muslims do not imagine that merely adhering to general principles is sufficient for them.

The phrase "يَفْسَحِ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ" (Allah will make space for you) has been interpreted by a group of commentators as the expansion of the gatherings in Paradise, which is the reward Allah gives to those who observe these etiquettes in this world. However, since the verse is absolute and has no restrictive conditions, it has a broad meaning and includes every kind of Divine expansion—whether in Paradise or in this world, in spirit and thought, in life and longevity, and in wealth and provision. It is not surprising for the grace of Allah to grant such a great reward for such a small act, for the reward is according to His generosity, not according to our deeds.

[^1]: The difference between the two expressions "Tafassahu" and "Fafas-hu"—one being from the Tafa''ul form and the other from the simple triliteral form—might be because the first implies a kind of effort (taking trouble), while the second is free from it. That is, whenever a speaker says, "Take the trouble to make room for the newcomer," they should make room without feeling it is a burden! (Reflect on this).

And since sometimes a gathering is so full that room cannot be found for others without some people standing up, or if a place is found, it is not suitable for them, the verse continues: "And when you are told, 'Arise,' then arise" (وَإِذَا قِيلَ انْشُزُوا فَانْشُزُوا) [^1]. Neither procrastinate nor be upset when you rise, for sometimes newcomers are more deserving of sitting than you due to extreme fatigue, old age, special respect they command, or other reasons. Here, those present must practice self-sacrifice (Ithar) and observe this Islamic etiquette, just as we read in the occasion of revelation that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered a group of those sitting near him to give their places to a group of newcomers who were veterans of Badr and surpassed others in knowledge and virtue.

Some commentators, noting that "Unshuzu" (Arise) is also absolute here, have interpreted it in a broader sense to include rising from a gathering, as well as rising for Jihad, prayer, and other good deeds. However, considering the previous phrase which has the restriction "in the assemblies" (fi al-majalis), it appears that this phrase is also restricted by the same, and its repetition was avoided due to the existing context.

Then, explaining the reward for fulfilling this Divine command, it adds: "If you do so, Allah will raise those of you who have believed and those who were given knowledge by many degrees" (يَرْفَعِ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ دَرَجَاتٍ) [^1]. This indicates that obeying these instructions is evidence of faith, knowledge, and awareness. It also points out that if the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered some to rise and give their places to newcomers, it was for a sacred Divine purpose and to respect those foremost in faith and knowledge.

The expression "Darajat" (degrees)—in its indefinite and plural form—refers to the great and lofty ranks that Allah grants to such individuals who possess both knowledge and faith together. In reality, those who make room for newcomers beside them have a degree, and those who practice self-sacrifice and give up their places to them, while benefiting from knowledge and insight, have even more degrees.

And since some perform these etiquettes with a willing heart and from the depths of their soul, while others do so with reluctance and displeasure, or for hypocrisy and show, the verse concludes: "And Allah is Aware of what you do" (وَاللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ).

[^1]: "Unshuzu" is derived from the root "Nashz" (rhyming with nasr), meaning high ground; hence, this word is also used to mean rising. A "Nashizah" woman is one who considers herself above obeying her husband. This word is also sometimes used to mean "bringing to life" because it causes one to rise up.

[^1]: "Yarfa'" in the above verse is in the jussive mood (majzum) because of the imperative form that precedes it, which effectively grants it the meaning of a conditional statement, and "Yarfa'" serves as the response to that condition.

Key Points

1. The Status of Scholars and Scientists

Although the verse was revealed regarding a specific case, it nonetheless carries a general meaning and shows that what raises a person's status before Allah are two things: 1. Faith, 2. Knowledge.

We know that the status of a "Martyr" (Shahid) in Islam is the highest status; yet, in a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "The superiority of the scholar over the martyr is by one degree, and the superiority of the martyr over the worshiper is by one degree... and the superiority of the scholar over the rest of the people is like my superiority over the least of them!" [^1]

In another Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever is overtaken by death while he is seeking knowledge, between him and the Prophets is but one degree!" [^2]

We know that on moonlit nights, especially the fourteenth night of the month when the "full moon" (Badr) is out, the stars fade in the light of the moon. Interestingly, in comparing the scholar and the worshiper, another Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله states: "The superiority of the scholar over the worshiper is like the superiority of the moon on the night of the full moon over the rest of the stars." [^3]

It is fascinating that the worshiper performs the worship which is the purpose of human creation, yet because the soul of worship is knowledge (Ma'rifah), the scholar has an extraordinary superiority over him.

What is mentioned in the above narrations regarding the superiority of the scholar over the worshiper refers to the vast distance between the two. Thus, in another Hadith, the difference between the two is mentioned as one hundred degrees instead of one, where the distance between each degree is the amount a fast horse travels in seventy years! [^4]

[^1] and [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 253. [^3] and [^4]: Jawami' al-Jami' (as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 264; Qurtubi, Vol. 9, p. 6470).

It is also clear that the status of intercession (Shafa'ah) on the Day of Resurrection does not belong to everyone, but rather to those near to Allah. However, in a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Three groups will intercede on the Day of Resurrection: the Prophets, then the scholars, then the martyrs!" [^1]

In reality, success on the path of perfection and gaining the pleasure of Allah and proximity to Him is dependent on two factors: faith and knowledge (or awareness and piety), neither of which is sufficient for guidance and victory without the other.

2. Etiquette of Gatherings

In the Holy Quran, alongside important matters, references are repeatedly made to the Islamic etiquettes of gatherings. These include the etiquette of greeting and entering a gathering, the etiquette of being invited to a meal, the etiquette of speaking with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the etiquette of making room for newcomers, especially those of virtue and those foremost in faith and knowledge. [^2] This clearly shows that the Quran values every subject in its proper place and never allows the human etiquettes of social interaction to be trampled upon due to individual indifference.

In books of Hadith, hundreds of narrations regarding the etiquette of social interaction with others have been narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Infallible Imams عليه السلام. The late "Sheikh Hurr al-Amili" collected them in the book Wasa'il al-Shia, Volume 8, in 166 chapters. The subtleties found in these narrations show to what extent Islam is meticulous and even strict in this regard.

In these narrations, even the manner of sitting, speaking, smiling, joking, feeding others, the manner of writing letters, and even the manner of looking at friends have been considered, and instructions have been given regarding each. Quoting them all would take us beyond the scope of this commentary; we will suffice here with only one Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام:

"Let there be gathered in your heart the need for people and the independence from them. Let your need for them be manifested in the softness of your speech and your good conduct, and let your independence from them be manifested in the preservation of your honor and the permanence of your dignity." [^1]

[^1]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 28, p. 26; Qurtubi, Vol. 9, p. 6470. [^2]: These instructions appear respectively in the following verses: Etiquette of greeting and Salam in An-Nisa: 86; Etiquette of invitation to a meal in Al-Ahzab: 53; Etiquette of speaking with the Prophet (ص) in Al-Hujurat: 2; and Etiquette of making room in the verses under discussion. [^1]: Wasa'il al-Shia, Vol. 8, p. 401.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا نَـٰجَيْتُمُ ٱلرَّسُولَ فَقَدِّمُوا۟ بَيْنَ يَدَىْ نَجْوَىٰكُمْ صَدَقَةً ۚ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ وَأَطْهَرُ ۚ فَإِن لَّمْ تَجِدُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٢)
O you who have faith! When you converse privately with the Apostle, offer a charity before your private talk. That is better for you and purer. But if you cannot afford [to make the offering], then Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (12)
ءَأَشْفَقْتُمْ أَن تُقَدِّمُوا۟ بَيْنَ يَدَىْ نَجْوَىٰكُمْ صَدَقَـٰتٍ ۚ فَإِذْ لَمْ تَفْعَلُوا۟ وَتَابَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمْ فَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (١٣)
Were you dismayed by having to offer charities before your private talks? Since you did not do it, and Allah has excused you [for not be able to comply], now maintain the prayer and pay the zakat, and obey Allah and His Apostle. Allah is well aware of what you do. (13)

Occasion of Revelation

The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan and a group of other renowned commentators have narrated the following regarding the occasion of the revelation of these verses: A group of wealthy individuals used to come to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and engage in private conversation (najwa) with him. (This act, in addition to consuming the precious time of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), caused concern among the underprivileged and created an unmerited distinction for the wealthy). At this point, Allah revealed the first of the above verses and commanded them to give charity to the needy before engaging in private conversation with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). When the wealthy saw this, they refrained from their private conversations. Then the second verse was revealed (reproaching them and abrogation the ruling of the first verse), granting permission for private conversation to everyone (provided that the conversation was regarding good deeds and obedience to the Lord). [^1] Some commentators have also explicitly stated that the goal of a group of those seeking private conversations was to gain superiority over others through this means. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), out of his particular nobility, did not prevent them despite being troubled by it, until the Quran forbade them from this practice. [^2]

Commentary

An Interesting Test

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 23; p. 447

A portion of the previous verses discussed the issue of private conversation (najwa); the verses under discussion are a continuation and completion of that same subject.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 252, and many other commentaries under the verses in question. [^2]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 28, p. 27.

First, He says: "O you who have believed, when you [wish to] privately consult the Messenger, present before your consultation a charity" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا نَاجَيْتُمُ الرَّسُولَ فَقَدِّمُوا بَيْنَ يَدَيْ نَجْوَاكُمْ صَدَقَةً).

As mentioned in the occasion of revelation, this command was because a group of people, especially the wealthy, were constantly bothering the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and engaging in private consultations with him—an act that caused grief to others or sought an unjustified distinction for themselves. Furthermore, it resulted in the wasting of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) precious time. The above ruling was revealed as a test for them, as well as a help for the poor and an effective means to reduce these disturbances.

Then He adds: "That is better for you and purer" (ذَلِكَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ وَأَطْهَرُ).

The "goodness" (khayr) of this charity was both for the affluent, as it resulted in spiritual reward, and for the needy, as it served as a means of assistance for them.

As for its being "purer" (athar), it was because it washed the hearts of the wealthy from the love of wealth and the hearts of the needy from resentment and distress. For when private consultation was no longer free and required giving charity, it naturally decreased—as indeed happened—and this was a kind of purification for the intellectual and social environment of the Muslims.

However, since the obligation of charity before private consultation, if universal, would have deprived the poor of presenting important issues or their needs to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in private, the end of the verse removes the ruling of charity from this group, stating:

"But if you do not find [the means], then indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful" (فَإِنْ لَمْ تَجِدُوا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

Thus, for those who had financial means, giving charity before private consultation was obligatory, while those who did not could consult with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) without it.

Interestingly, the above command had a remarkable effect and became a fascinating test; everyone except one person refrained from giving charity and private consultation. That person was the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام). It was here that what needed to be clarified became clear, and the Muslims learned the lesson they were meant to take from this command. Therefore, the next verse was revealed, abrogating this ruling and saying: "Have you feared that you should present charities before your consultation?" (أَأَشْفَقْتُمْ أَنْ تُقَدِّمُوا بَيْنَ يَدَيْ نَجْوَاكُمْ صَدَقَاتٍ).

It became evident that the love of wealth in your hearts is greater than the desire for private consultation with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). It also became clear that in these private consultations, vital matters were often not being discussed; otherwise, what would have prevented this group from giving a charity and then consulting, especially since no specific amount was determined for the charity and they could have solved this problem with a small sum?

Then He adds: "Then when you do not do it and Allah has forgiven you, then establish prayer and give zakah and obey Allah and His Messenger. And Allah is Acquainted with what you do" (فَإِذْ لَمْ تَفْعَلُوا وَتَابَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمْ فَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكَاةَ وَأَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَاللَّهُ خَبِيرٌ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ).

The expression "Allah has forgiven you" (taba Allahu 'alaykum) indicates that they had committed a sin in their previous private consultations, whether due to ostentation and hypocrisy, or by troubling the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), or by causing distress to the poor believers.

Although this verse does not explicitly mention the permissibility of private consultation after this incident, the phrasing of the verse indicates that the previous ruling was lifted.

However, the call to establish prayer, pay zakah, and obey Allah and His Messenger is due to its importance, and it is also an indication that if you engage in private consultation after this, it must be in the direction of great Islamic goals and in the path of obedience to Allah and His Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله).

Key Points

1- The Only One Who Acted Upon the Verse of Charity and Consultation

Most Shiite and Sunni commentators have written that the only person who acted upon this verse was the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام). As Tabarsi narrates from the Imam (عليه السلام) himself:

"There is a verse in the Book of Allah that no one acted upon before me, and no one will act upon after me. I had a dinar which I exchanged for ten dirhams, and whenever I wanted to consult privately with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), I would give a dirham in charity." [^1] The same meaning has been narrated by Shawkani from Abd al-Razzaq, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh. [^2] Fakhr al-Razi also narrated this hadith—that the only one who acted upon the above verse was Ali (عليه السلام)—from a group of traditionists from Ibn Abbas. [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Tabari, Vol. 28, p. 15. [^2]: Al-Bayan fi Tafsir al-Quran, Vol. 1, p. 375. Sayyid Qutb also narrated this tradition in Fi Zilal al-Quran, Vol. 8, p. 21.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, multiple narrations are mentioned under the above verses to the same effect. [^2] In the commentary Ruh al-Bayan, it is narrated from Abdullah, the son of Umar ibn al-Khattab, that he used to say: "Ali (عليه السلام) had three virtues; if I possessed even one of them, it would be dearer to me than red camels (this expression was used among Arabs to refer to the most precious of wealth, and was used as a proverb when describing something extremely valuable): first, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) marrying Fatima (سلام الله عليها) to him; second, giving him the banner on the day of Khaybar; and third, the Verse of Private Consultation (Najwa)!" [^3] The establishment of this great virtue for Ali (عليه السلام) is found in most books of commentary and hadith and is so famous and well-known that it needs no further elaboration.

2- The Philosophy of Legislating and Abrogating the Ruling of Charity Before Consultation

Why was the obligation of charity before private consultation with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) legislated and then abrogated after a short period?

[^1]: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 29, p. 271. [^2]: al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 185. [^3]: Tafsir Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 406 (This hadith was cited by Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Zamakhshari in al-Kashshaf, and Qurtubi in al-Jami' under the verses in question).

The answer to this question can be clearly derived from the evidence present in the verse itself and its occasion of revelation. The goal was a test for those boastful individuals who expressed a special devotion to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) through this means. It became clear that these expressions of devotion existed only when the consultation was free; however, when it required the sacrifice of some wealth, they fell silent.

Beyond this, this ruling left a lasting impression on the Muslims, showing that unless there is a necessity, one should not take up the precious time of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and other great Islamic leaders with private consultations, nor provide a means for the people's dissatisfaction. In reality, it was a control mechanism for future consultations.

Therefore, the said ruling had a temporary nature from the beginning, and after its objective was secured, it was abrogated. This is because its continuation would have created a problem, as sometimes necessary matters arose that needed to be discussed privately with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). If the ruling of charity had remained, perhaps these necessities would have been lost, and individuals or the Islamic society would have suffered.

Generally, in cases of abrogation (naskh), the ruling always has a limited and temporary nature from the start, even if people were occasionally unaware of this and perceived it as permanent.

3- Was This a Virtue?

Undoubtedly, Ali (عليه السلام) was not among the wealthy companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله); he lived a simple and ascetic life. Nevertheless, out of respect for this divine command, he gave charity several times during that short period and discussed necessary matters with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) through private consultation. As we have said, this matter is certain among commentators and masters of hadith.

However, some, while accepting this fact, insist on denying its status as a virtue.

Among their claims is that if the senior companions did not undertake this act, it was because they saw no need for it, or they did not have enough time, or they thought it might cause distress to the poor and alarm the wealthy! Therefore, it is not considered a virtue for Ali (عليه السلام) nor does it imply a loss of virtue for others! [^1] But it seems they have not reflected on the text of the second verse, where Allah says by way of reproach: "Have you feared that you should present charities before your consultation?"—meaning, did you fear poverty and act miserly such that you did not give charity before consultation? Even at the end of the verse, He uses the term "repentance" (tawbah), which apparently refers to this same meaning. From this expression, it is clear that acting upon the charity and consultation with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was a desirable act; otherwise, there would be no reproach or mention of repentance.

Undoubtedly, a group of prominent companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) had private consultations with him before this incident (since ordinary and distant people were less likely to do such a thing). Yet these same well-known companions refrained from this act after the command of charity. The only one who respected this command and put it into practice was Ali (عليه السلام). Why should we not accept the apparent meaning of the verses and the narrations regarding this matter—which are recorded in various Islamic books—and instead ignore a reality with weak and baseless conjectures? Why not join our voice with Abdullah ibn Umar, who ranked this virtue alongside the marriage to Fatima (عليه السلام) and the banner-bearing on the day of the conquest of Khaybar, and considered it superior to "red camels"?

[^1]: Refer to Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi and Ruh al-Bayan under the verses in question.

4- The Duration of the Ruling and the Amount of Charity

Regarding how long the above ruling—the obligation of charity before private consultation with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله)—lasted before being abrogated, there are various reports: some mention one hour, some one night, and some ten days. The third view seems most plausible, as one hour or one night would never be sufficient for such a test; they could have excused themselves by saying no occasion for consultation arose in that short time. However, a period of ten days can clarify the facts and provide a basis for the blame and reproach of those who failed to comply.

As for the amount of charity, neither the verse mentions a specific amount nor do the Islamic narrations, although it can be inferred from the action of Ali (عليه السلام) that even one dirham was sufficient.

Ayahs

۞ أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ تَوَلَّوْا۟ قَوْمًا غَضِبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مَّا هُم مِّنكُمْ وَلَا مِنْهُمْ وَيَحْلِفُونَ عَلَى ٱلْكَذِبِ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ (١٤)
